evals.prompt: We're 18 months into a $80M DT program. Diagnose why we feel stuck.
evals.expected_output: Should run failure-pattern diagnostic against 7 common patterns: (1) Big-bang program (likely — $80M, 3+ years suggests one big bet rather than portfolio); (2) Tech-led not business-led (common — likely scope is 'implement [platform]' not 'achieve customer outcome'); (3) No portfolio shape (likely — single program, not H1/H2/H3 mix); (4) Centralized decisions (likely — slow approval, distant from where work happens); (5) Talent gap unacknowledged (likely — hired CDO, didn't upskill 5,000 people who do the work); (6) Culture change skipped (likely — assumed new tools produce new behaviors); (7) No success criteria (likely — 'transform' defined fuzzily). Plus apply Kane et al.'s maturity-stage diagnostic — likely Stage 2 (Siloed) or stuck transitioning to Stage 3 (Partially Synchronized). Recommendations: (a) Reframe the singular program as a portfolio — H1 (foundation modernization), H2 (new digital businesses for existing customers), H3 (transformational bets); (b) Distribute decision rights to product/domain teams; (c) Talent strategy paired with capability roadmap; (d) Visible quick wins in next 90 days to rebuild momentum; (e) Cultural mechanisms (rituals, decision rights, metrics) installed in parallel. Note: 18-month stuck pattern is recoverable if leadership commits to portfolio reframe; 24-30 months stuck is much harder. Time-sensitive.

evals.prompt: Reframe this big-bang DT program as a portfolio. What are the H1/H2/H3 bets? Context: 'Modernize our core platform, build a customer-360 view, launch self-service portal, and transform our service operations.'
evals.expected_output: Should structure the four 'transformation' initiatives across horizons with appropriate governance: H1 (defend/extend core, 0-12 months, stage-gate, NPV-justified): core platform modernization (this is mostly H1 — replatforming with similar functional outcomes; not transformation). H2 (build emerging businesses, 1-3 years, discovery-driven w/ stage-gate, validated learning + early revenue): customer-360 view (H2 if framed as enabling NEW customer experiences/services; H1 if just dashboard); self-service portal (H2 — likely reaching new customer segments or replacing high-cost service); service operations modernization (mostly H1 — efficiency play). H3 (create new business models, 3+ years, real options, learning ROI): NONE in the current scope. Recommendation: (1) acknowledge the program is mostly H1 with one H2 — that's modernization, not transformation; (2) consider whether H3 bets should be added to make this real transformation (e.g., AI-native service models, platform business model, ecosystem plays); (3) governance per horizon — H1 gets stage-gate and NPV; H2 gets discovery-driven with pilot adoption metrics; H3 gets real options and learning ROI. Time horizons revised: H1 work in 12-18 months; H2 work in 18-36 months; H3 (if added) in 36+ months. Note: most 'transformations' are modernizations dressed up — be honest about what's real.

evals.prompt: Apply the Rogers five-domains diagnostic to our retail client. What did they miss?
evals.expected_output: Should structure five-domains review for the client. Customers: have they shifted from 'mass market' to 'customer network'? Most retail DT misses this — they keep mass-market campaign architecture even with personalization tools. Competition: have they expanded their competitive frame beyond their industry (DTC, marketplaces, content platforms)? Most retail DT defines competition as other retailers, missing the asymmetric platform threat. Data: have they built data products, or just data infrastructure? Most retail DT digitizes operations but doesn't productize data. Innovation: have they shifted from intuition+market-research to rapid testing? Most retail DT preserves the seasonal merchandising rhythm, no validated-learning loops. Value: have they reframed the value proposition for the customer? Most retail DT preserves the original value prop, just digitized. Likely findings: 'they did Innovation and Data domains (rapid testing infrastructure, data warehouse) but didn't reframe Customers, Competition, or Value.' Recommendation: pair the technology-stack work they've done with strategy reframes across all 5 domains; without all 5, the DT remains incomplete and underdelivers. Reference Rogers's specific value-proposition pivots (targeting, focusing, refocusing, repositioning, broadening, repurposing). Cross-link with cx-personalization-strategist and consulting-digital-strategist for execution.

evals.prompt: Our CDO has responsibility without authority. Recommend the org redesign.
evals.expected_output: Should diagnose the CDO trap: hired with broad mandate but without (a) decision rights, (b) budget authority, (c) hire/fire authority over digital talent across the org, (d) reporting line that gives leverage. Common patterns: CDO reports to CIO (limits to tech focus, removes business authority); CDO has 'strategy' authority but operating BUs control execution; CDO has accountability but no budget independence. Redesign options: (1) CDO as Enabler — explicit charter that the CDO sets digital standards, owns capability building (CoEs), runs central platforms; operating BUs own their digital roadmaps within those standards; CDO reports to CEO; (2) CDO as Owner — explicit P&L authority over digital revenue lines; operating BU partnerships clearly defined; (3) CDO retired — distribute the role across Chief Customer Officer (customer-facing digital), Chief Operating Officer (operational digital), Chief Marketing Officer (marketing digital); pair with Centers of Excellence; CDO becomes a temporary scaffolding role. Recommendation depends on org maturity (Kane et al. stages): Stage 1-2 orgs benefit from a strong CDO; Stage 3-4 orgs distribute the role. For most enterprises, CDO-as-Enabler is the right move — central capability, distributed execution. Cross-link with consulting-management-consultant for org structure and innovation-leadership-coach for sponsor / capital work for the CDO.

evals.prompt: We don't need transformation, we need modernization. How do I make that case to the board?
evals.expected_output: Should support the framing: most 'transformations' are modernizations, and naming accurately saves years and millions. Structure for the board case: (1) Definitions — Modernization: same business, better infrastructure (cost-out, capability uplift, platform replacement). Transformation: new business models, new customer-value reframes, new competitive position. Different governance, metrics, talent, runway; (2) Diagnosis of current state — what specifically are we trying to change? If it's mostly H1 (efficiency, foundation), that's modernization. If it requires H2/H3 bets with new business models or new customer segments, that's transformation; (3) Cost of mis-naming — modernization run as transformation: over-invests in change management theater, underinvests in execution rigor; produces transformation-narrative without transformation-results. Transformation run as modernization: under-invests in cultural and operating-model work; produces modernization-results when transformation was needed; (4) The strategic question — does the firm need to modernize to defend, or transform to thrive? Most boards accept 'we need to modernize aggressively to defend our position; transformation is a separate decision and we don't recommend it now' — that's an honest, defensible answer. (5) Recommendation framework — if board insists on transformation language, stress-test by asking 'what new business model are we building? What new customer segment are we serving? What about our current model are we abandoning?' If answers are weak, it's modernization. Reference *Why Digital Transformations Fail* on naming honestly.

evals.prompt: Score our maturity stage on Kane et al.'s 4-stage model. We have a CDO, some digital products, lots of data infrastructure, partial AI/ML deployment, but most decisions still flow through corporate IT.
evals.expected_output: Should run the 4-stage diagnostic against four differentiators (strategy alignment, leadership, talent, culture). Likely diagnosis: Stage 2 (Developing) with partial Stage 3 traits. Specifically: (1) Strategy alignment — depends on whether 'digital strategy' is communicated organization-wide and internalized, or lives in the CDO's deck only. Likely: explicit at exec level, not internalized broadly; that's Stage 2; (2) Leadership — 'most decisions flow through corporate IT' = centralized digital leadership = Stage 1-2 pattern; Stage 3+ has distributed digital leadership across every domain leader; (3) Talent — having a CDO doesn't equal Stage 3+ talent; the question is whether the org systematically builds adaptive digital talent across functions. Likely Stage 2 — pockets of capability; (4) Culture — having data infrastructure doesn't equal Stage 3+ culture; the question is whether decision rights, experimentation rituals, and failure tolerance are distributed mechanisms. Likely Stage 2 — intent without mechanisms. Recommendation: focus on the move from Stage 2 to Stage 3 — distributed digital leadership, talent investment beyond CDO, cultural mechanisms beyond infrastructure. The leap from Stage 2 to Stage 3 is structural (org redesign, mechanism design), not incremental. Time horizon: 18-30 months with executive commitment. Reference Kane et al. core finding: technology choice is not the differentiator; strategy/leadership/talent/culture are.

evals.prompt: We've been through 3 'digital transformations' in 8 years. None worked. Diagnose the recurring failure.
evals.expected_output: Should treat as a systemic-pattern problem, not a single-program problem. Recurring DT failure across 3 programs in 8 years suggests the failure mode is org-cultural, not program-specific. Likely structural causes: (1) Mandate confusion — board/CEO commits to 'transform' rhetorically but doesn't actually want transformation (which would disrupt existing power, comp, and strategy); (2) CFO governance — programs are run on H1 metrics (cost-out, ROI) which kills H2/H3 work systematically; (3) Sponsor turnover — average sponsor tenure shorter than DT program duration, so sponsors change before programs land; (4) Incentive misalignment — leaders are rewarded for not-rocking-the-boat; transformation rocks the boat; (5) Consultant rotation — each program brings in different external advisors, destroying continuity and learning; (6) Talent flight — best talent leaves between programs; institutional learning lost. Recommendation: stop running 'transformation programs' as such. Instead: (a) embed digital-portfolio thinking into ongoing strategy work (not a special program); (b) make digital capability building a permanent operating commitment, not project; (c) invest in cultural mechanisms that survive sponsor turnover; (d) build a leadership cohort with multi-year commitment (not just a CEO and CDO). The framing change is from 'transformation as event' to 'transformation as ongoing organizational competence.' Reference Kane et al.'s Stage 4 (Maturing+) — culture matches strategy, talent flows, learning loops at every level.

evals.prompt: How does AI-native operations change DT thinking? Should we treat AI as another DT initiative or differently?
evals.expected_output: Should treat AI-native as both a within-DT-portfolio question and a separate strategic question. Within DT: AI-native operations are H2/H3 bets that should sit in a digital portfolio with horizon-appropriate governance — discovery-driven planning, real options for novel applications, learning ROI for early stage. Treating AI as a singular 'AI program' replicates the big-bang DT failure pattern. Separately strategic: AI-native operations may require organizational changes that DT didn't (different talent pool, different ethics review, different data/privacy infrastructure, different regulatory exposure, different change management because impact on workforce is more direct). Specific recommendations: (1) Portfolio inclusion — AI bets get same governance as other digital portfolio bets, with horizon-appropriate metrics; (2) Capability investment — separate, multi-year (AI talent, MLOps, data products, ethics review); (3) Risk frameworks — AI-specific (model risk, hallucination, bias, regulatory) layered on top of standard DT risk; (4) Change management — AI affects job design directly; OCM workstream with employee co-creation, not just communication; (5) Vendor strategy — AI vendor mix is volatile; build optionality, avoid lock-in early; (6) Strategy reframe — AI-native enables new business models (Doblin Type 1 + Type 4 + Type 5 combinations); typologist work surfaces these. Cross-link with consulting-digital-strategist (engagement framing), software-engineering-ai-architect (capability), behavioral-economics for adoption design, consulting-change-management-advisor for OCM.
